title: Type Juggling & Casting
description: Understand how PHP automatically converts types and how to force conversions.

slides.title: PHP is Loosely Typed
slides.content:
# No Need to Declare Types

In PHP, you don't have to tell the language what type a variable is. PHP figures it out from the context.

Moreover, PHP automatically converts types when needed. This is called **type juggling**.

Example:
```php
<?php
$number = "10";   // string
$sum = $number + 5; // PHP converts "10" to integer, result is 15 (integer)
?>
```

slides.title: Type Juggling in Action
slides.content:
```php
<?php
$a = "10";       // string
$b = 5;          // integer
$c = $a + $b;    // string to int conversion, $c = 15 (int)
$d = $a . $b;    // concatenation: integer to string, $d = "105" (string)

echo $c;         // 15
echo $d;         // 105
?>
```

slides.title: Type Casting
slides.content:
# Forcing a Type

If you want to explicitly convert a value to a specific type, you can use **type casting**.

Syntax: `(type) $variable`

Common casts:
*   `(int)` or `(integer)` ŌĆō to integer
*   `(float)` or `(double)` ŌĆō to float
*   `(string)` ŌĆō to string
*   `(bool)` or `(boolean)` ŌĆō to boolean
*   `(array)` ŌĆō to array
*   `(object)` ŌĆō to object

slides.title: Casting Examples
slides.content:
```php
<?php
$num = "123.45";

$intVal = (int) $num;     // 123
$floatVal = (float) $num; // 123.45
$boolVal = (bool) $num;   // true (any non-empty, non-zero becomes true)

var_dump($intVal);   // int(123)
var_dump($floatVal); // float(123.45)
var_dump($boolVal);  // bool(true)
?>
```

quizData.question: What is the result of `echo "5" * "2";`?
quizData.explanation: The `*` operator expects numbers, so PHP converts both strings to integers and multiplies, giving 10.
